overview: Performs search and replace actions on either the text units (when receiving filter events) or the full content of input documents (when receiving raw documents). Patterns are processed in the order they are declared, and both literal and Java regular expression modes are supported. The step sends back the same type it receives — updated filter events or an updated raw document.

regEx.help:
Enables Java regular expression syntax for all search patterns. When off, search patterns are literal text with only basic escape sequences (`\n`, `\r`, `\t`, `\\`, `\uHHHH`).

When on, replacement strings support `$N` for back-references to captured groups (`$1`, `$2`, etc.) and `\$` for a literal dollar sign. See Java [`Matcher.replaceAll()`](http://java.sun.com/javase/6/docs/api/java/util/regex/Matcher.html#replaceAll(java.lang.String)) for details.
notes: Applies globally to **all** patterns in the list — you cannot mix literal and regex patterns.
In all modes (regex on or off), use `\\` to represent a literal backslash. For `\C` where `C` is not a recognized escape, the result is the literal character (e.g., `\*` → `*`).

dotAll.help: Changes the meaning of `.` so it matches **every** character including `\n` (line-feed). Without this, `.` matches everything except line-feed.

ignoreCase.help: Makes pattern matching case-insensitive. For example, `bear` matches `Bear`, `BEAR`, and `bear`.

multiLine.help: Changes the meaning of `^` and `$` so they match at the beginning and end of **any line**, not just the beginning and end of the whole string.

target.help: Performs search and replace on the **target** content of text units. Ignored when processing a raw document.
notes: When working on target content, you may need to use the **Create Target Step** before this step to ensure there is content in the target.

source.help: Performs search and replace on the **source** content of text units. Ignored when processing a raw document.

replaceALL.help:
Replaces **all** matches found. When disabled, only the **first** match is replaced.

- **Text units mode**: replaces all or only one occurrence *per text unit*
- **Raw document mode**: replaces all or only one occurrence *for the whole file*

replacementsPath.help:
Path to an external tab-delimited, UTF-8-encoded text file with two columns: search strings (column 1) and replacement strings (column 2).

This file is processed **after** all patterns in the main list have been applied. Replacements from this file are limited to **literal (non-regex) substring** searches only.
examples: `${rootDir}/my-replacements.txt` — tab-delimited file in the pipeline root directory

logPath.help: Path where the replacement log file will be written when logging is enabled.

saveLog.help: Saves a log of all replacements performed to the file specified in the log path.

count.help: Internal counter tracking the number of replacements performed. Not typically set by the user.

limitations: When processing a raw document, the **entire file content is loaded into memory**, which may cause problems with very large documents.
The external replacements file (`replacementsPath`) only supports **literal (non-regex) substring** searches — regex is not available for external file replacements.
The `source` and `target` options are ignored when processing a raw document (the whole file is processed regardless).
processingNotes: Patterns are processed **in declaration order**. Longer patterns should be placed first if there is a chance of overlapping matches.
The external replacements file is processed **after** all in-table patterns.
When receiving **filter events**, search/replace operates on text unit content. When receiving a **raw document**, it operates on the entire file as plain text (no conversion of escaped characters).
The step sends back the same event type it receives — filter events in, filter events out; raw document in, raw document out.

examples.title: Escape sequences in literal mode
examples.description:
Special escape sequences available in both literal and regex modes for search and replace patterns:

| Escape | Meaning |
|--------|----------|
| `\n` | line-feed |
| `\r` | carriage-return |
| `\t` | tab |
| `\\` | literal backslash |
| `\uHHHH` | Unicode character (hex) |

Additionally in **regex replacement** strings: `$N` = captured group N, `\$` = literal dollar sign.

examples.title: Regex with group back-references
examples.description:
With **regular expressions** enabled, captured groups in the search pattern can be referenced in the replacement using `$1`, `$2`, etc.
